title: Nikhil Kamath x NZ PM Christopher Luxon | People by WTF Ep #7

chunks.text: Are we set, guys?
How should I address you? Prime Minister...
-Chris is fine. -Yeah? You sure?
Absolutely.
So, Chris, I'm not gonna go the usual path of an interview.
-I think this is more a conversation. -Yeah.
And I went to the match recently, the India-New Zealand cricket final.
-Oh, my goodness. -Yeah.
I was hoping you weren't gonna--
This podcast was going so well, until you had to go mention that.
Hi, Chris. Thank you for doing this.
It's so awesome to be with you,
and with such a celebrated entrepreneur like yourself.
So, it's great to be with you.
Absolutely. Absolutely.
Well, welcome to India.
I saw bits of you going to different places in India.
How has that been so far?
I saw you with the Prime Minister.
I saw you at the Gurudwara, at a cricket game.
Yeah, it's been absolutely magical.
I mean, we've had a fantastic four to five days here in India.
And essentially, India is a place that I first got to know
when I was working at Unilever
and we had a very large business here called Hindustan Lever.
And, as a result, I used to come here in my, probably, mid to late twenties.
So when I think about how it has progressed
from when I first visited to where I am today,
it's just so inspiring.
I mean, it's such an ambitious, aspirational,
can-do, positive kind of place.
And you just see the trajectory that it has been on,
that it is now, and that it's on now for the future.
And it's just... it's all about growth,
and it's all about energy and drive and determination.
And it's just really, really contagious.
So, it's awesome to be here.
I brought the biggest delegation of any Prime Minister
on any visit that we've had from New Zealand.
And I had a big business delegation, and also a community delegation,
and also our indigenous kapa haka team, performing arts team, with us as well.
So it's been a very full on set of four or five days,
but these are two great countries that should have a closer relationship
and be a much deeper, broader relationship.
I saw this member of parliament,
I'd like to say, performing the haka at parliament.
-Yes. -It looked so good.
Can you tell us a bit about that? What was that?
Well, look, it's really...
you know, our kapa haka, which is our Maori performing arts.
Maori are the indigenous people of New Zealand.
-My brother has a Maori tattoo. -He does? Yeah, yeah.
They do it with a nib, right? Like, just poking?
Yeah, you got it, you got it.
And they're just incredible because actually, right from, you know...
New Zealand's been a very innovative country
if you think about these Maori Polynesian navigators
that navigated by the stars
and came through the Pacific Ocean and into New Zealand.
And so, it's actually awesome to be able to bring them with me here
because what I really appreciate is
when you run into Indians, they've got huge appreciation for culture,
your own culture, it's thousands of years of civilization.
It's just incredibly mesmerizing and interesting.
And I think, yeah, there's huge respect for Maori culture as well.
And it's a great way also to make the connections
that are human connections, like, on a people-to-people level.
So I'm really proud of bringing some of our best exponents of us to India,
and I actually think they are due to, at some point,
go perform in front of the Taj Mahal later on this weekend,
which-- that must be pretty awesome.
Looking at you from this close, your eyes are a very interesting color.
-Are they? -Yeah, what color are they?
I think they're just... I think they're green.
I think they might be light green.
So, yeah, they seem to work.
I seem to be able to see things pretty well, which is the main thing.
So I was going through your social media bio,
and amongst other things, it said,
first a husband, then a father,
then a brother, then a son,
-and then New Zealand Prime Minister. -Yes.
Is that how you view life?
Well, I think, yeah, I've had the fortunate position
of meeting lots of interesting people over my career in business,
and, in the last four years, in politics.
And I always think it's not what you do,
it's actually who you are that matters much more.
If you put all of your identity into a title
and into some perceived status, that then will come and go.
And if you put all your identity into that stuff,
rather than actually who you are as an individual or a person
and what kind of person you're becoming, it's much more interesting.
You know, at some point, I will no longer be Prime Minister of New Zealand,
and it will be a tremendous honor, and it's a great responsibility,
and I absolutely love this job, but I'm not defined by the job.
And I think I've seen a lot of people get very defined
by their job titles on their business cards.
And when that goes away, they shatter themselves
because they put all their identity in store into that title.
So that's all I think is just my approach to life in general.
Can I also ask why husband and father first,
and then brother and son,
'cause those are older relationships in a way?
Yeah, it's just, I mean, we're very close with my wife's family and my own family,
but I was very fortunate that I met my wife, Amanda,
when I was 15 years old.
And we were dating at 19 and married at 23.
And then we traveled the world together with my business career,
and then had our children overseas.
And so, I'm always very grateful that I married up
and get to do life with Amanda as my life partner, it's pretty special.
And then, obviously, I've got two children, William and Olivia,
and we've been a very tight foursome
because, you know, I left New Zealand as a 24 year old.
I was overseas for 16 years, and we had our children overseas
without a lot of family support around us, obviously.
And we became a very tight unit.
But equally, I've got two younger brothers and an awesome mum and dad,
and who we're all very close with as well.
So, family's everything, right?
I mean, it's everything. It's where we learn how to behave.
It's how we learn to...
It's actually the building block of our community.
And so, yeah, I always think, at the end of the day,
I wanna be able to get to 90 years old, look back on my life,
and be able to put my arm around my adult son
and say, you know, our relationships are awesome.
You know, what you've achieved in life will be interesting to a point,
but as I keep saying, who you are and the relationships that you have
is what life's ultimately all about.
And I will not ask you many of the questions
that you have been asked already.
Maybe the Khalistani thing or the nuclear thing
or New Zealand's relationship with China, India and so on and so forth.
My audience is largely people who are working in a company
or looking to start a company.
I'm very interested in your transition from Unilever to Air New Zealand
to being a politician today.
That seems to be happening everywhere in the world right now as a trend.
I could draw an analogy to Trump, and I wanna ask you questions about that,
how that transition felt like.
Would you like to begin by describing it in your own words
and tell me, A, was it worth it?
And B, why the transition?
Yeah, look, it is tremendously worth it.
I mean, the privilege of being able to lead a country
is really very, very special.
You know, it's incredibly challenging,
but it's also, you know, very, very rewarding.
And you want to go to that job
because you've bought into this notion of public service,
and actually, you're there to actually improve the livelihoods of your citizens.
And that's very much my motivation.
So, I'm not a career politician, which is very unusual in New Zealand.
I have come from outside the system, but I actually think that's really good
because you have a different outsider perspective
on what needs to change or what you want to do
or how you think the country can be better.
And I just feel New Zealand has so much potential
that actually there's a real opportunity for us
to actually get things sorted and solved for people
so they actually get a better quality of life.
And actually, I genuinely buy into that.
With respect to the business to the politics transition,
I always felt I wanted to go do something else
before I'd come to political life because I--
You always think that, that you'd come to political life?
Yeah, at some point, I always thought I would or I'd like to think I would
or have the opportunity to do so.
But essentially, yeah, the reason for that is very simply that
I really think actually doing something else
before you come to political life is often very, very helpful
because you understand more of the world
and you understand more of what people are,
how you can actually help people, how you can deal with them.
So, for me, yeah, having a business career was something I always wanted to do.
But I always think that the skills from some of that
is very transferable into my job.
It's not the same thing, running a company as it is a country.
It's actually completely different and very different on a number of levels.
But the principles by which you do that, the way that you lead,
the way that you build a team of ministers to actually deliver the agenda,
to actually get things done is very, very similar.
And so, some of the same principles
that I learnt in business actually are very applicable
now into this role as prime minister.
A lot of people ask me if I will ever be in politics.
Yes. And what do you think?
I have no intent whatsoever, at least not today.
But what is one skill that is serving you well in politics
that you picked up in business?
And what is the one thing that is not working out for you?
Well, I think the first thing is this notion of
"How do you protect and enhance the good things
that have either happened in a country or a company?"
That principle is the same.
And then, also, how do you provoke and stimulate change?
And actually, if it's all provoke and stimulate change,
turning everything upside down, that never works.
But if it's also just more incrementalism
and more of the same, that also doesn't work.
And I think the task of a leader, whether you're a CEO or prime minister,
is to find that balance, protect and enhance the good stuff,
make sure you stimulate and provoke change on new things
that will actually move the agenda forward,
move a country or move an organization forward.
But coming into politics, the way I looked at it
was that I was really...
you know, I'd gone from playing cricket to now playing hockey.
You know, like it's actually, you had to start from the beginning
and not come in assumptive
that just because you've been successful in one sphere of life,
that you're immediately gonna be successful in another sphere.
And so, actually being prepared to start at the beginning
and actually be humble and learn what you need to know
in order to help you move forward in your political career
was very important to me.
So, yeah, that's how I approached life,
was just be curious about it and actually learn
and focus on learning and the rest takes care of itself.
So you're saying curiosity and learning
that you learnt in business serves you well, is good for politics?
Yeah, I think when I've sat down with...
Yeah, I remember sitting in Chicago, where I lived for a long time,
with a bunch of quite high-powered CEOs
and realizing that actually, academically, maybe they were all B-plus type students,
you know, smart enough people.
But when you say why are they successful and why are others not?
I think it comes down to... do you have really good character
in terms of a good, even temperament?
I'm not someone who goes really high or low,
I don't catastrophize when things go badly.
I don't go bananas when everything's going well,
I just stay pretty even at an emotional level
and a temperament level, but I think the character really matters.
I think, curiosity, that you're keen
to keep learning new things is really important,
and you never stop learning,
and you've actually got to be smart enough to learn new things.
I think, you know, communication skills are critical,
and also, the other piece is conflict management skills.
We're in the people business in politics, actually,
and that requires good levels of EQ
in terms of managing egos and personalities and people
and all those things that you do to build good teams.
You know, that's very transferable into political life as well.
So, you know, I think those are the skills
that actually say, you know, yes, I've got commercial skills,
yes, I have a good economics orientation
at a time when our economy's been somewhat challenged in recent years,
we're coming through that, but, you know, that's been quite helpful,
having that business commercial orientation.
But I think the bigger piece is actually just the leadership skills that you have
from one field to another are transferable
if you can think through how to deploy them
in often quite a different context.
And when you say you don't emote the highs and lows,
I'm assuming you're not as reactive as the next person.
Are you not feeling the emotion or are you not projecting the emotion?
No, I mean, I know I feel it,
but the question is, I also, you know, do things,
like, I'm fully informed about social media,
but I'm not consumed by it
because, otherwise, there's so many critics out there
that you'd never get out of bed in the morning, right,
if your whole life was defined
by what comments are said about you on social media.
So that's a way in which you make sure that you're not bounced around by people
who you may or may not choose to take advice from.
And so, you know, that to me is,
I've got a very good set of friends and mentors
and family around me that keep me really grounded.
And so, for me, I'm just not, I'm a person who's always believed
that, you know, like good sports teams, you know, when you say good...
I study a lot of coaches and captains of sports teams
because it's the same dynamic.
In politics, people often think of it as an individual ego-driven game,
but actually, I've determined,
and the work that we've done with our team is,
now we're in a team game.
I can play the position on the team. I may be opening bat or whatever,
but I need a really fast bowler, I need a spin bowler,
I need, you know, I need different people on the team
that can play their position in a world-class era
and do a really good job.
And it's about my job as captain as to make sure I get the right people
in the right places on the right assignments
with great clarity about what we're trying to achieve
and what success looks like.
This not allowing critique or social media affect you
is a superpower in today's world, almost.
When I look back in time in history,
a king's son always knew he would be king one day
and was probably prepared for the good and bad
of being in that place where everybody has an opinion.
I'd love to learn this.
For somebody who is a first-generation politician,
who, I could maybe presume, did not particularly know for sure
that you would be a politician up until a few years ago,
how did you inculcate that skill of not letting the criticism affect you?
Well, I think it just comes back to life, whether you're a community leader,
whether you're a political leader or a business leader.
It's the same principle, which is that,
ultimately, I am a person who gets very connected
to the mission and purpose that I'm doing.
I came to political life.
I came home from overseas, back home to New Zealand.
I came into politics because I genuinely am connected
to the mission of "New Zealand's an awesome country
and it's got so much more potential", and I genuinely want to do that.
Beyond that, I listen to what people have.
I take advice.
That's a very important thing to be able to take on board feedback.
But I'm not gonna sit there and get bounced around
by some comment on social media, for goodness sake.
You know, honestly, everyone can have their opinions,
but not all opinions are equal.
I choose not to take advice from people on social media if I don't want to.
So I get to choose that.
There is a moment between stimulus and response
as to how you choose to respond to that stimulus.
So, for me, I'm informed about what's going on,
but I don't need to be consumed,
and I'm not on my phone, looking at what everyone's saying.
And I think that's really sad, when people get that way,
because, actually, that's the real human to human connections
and relationships that you want to be investing your time in.
We all have 24 hours in a day, and the question is,
what do you do with your time?
And I think when you get to the end of life,
I've spent time talking to people in their 70s and 80s and 90s
and asking about what their life's like.
It's about the quality of the relationships that they have,
not how much money they've made or what status they've had.
That's all very interesting, but actually, it's the quality of the relationships
and the impact you've had with people that will ultimately matter.
I think that's very well said. A lot of people can learn from that.
When I think about who could be politicians in society next,
I'm thinking artists, entrepreneurs, business people.
If I were to assume that money is a precondition to success in politics,
the logical path would almost be for businessmen to become politicians
like we see happening in different pockets of the world.
Also, excellence in business, in managing people, like you said,
is a big talent that translates into politics as well.
Do you agree with the role of money in politics?
No. I mean, we come from a political system in New Zealand
where money, we have very tight laws around campaign financing.
And so we don't have the challenges
that I observe in other countries around the world
or other democracies where money buys influence.
We have very strict, independent electoral commission laws.
I think, in an electoral campaign,
you can't spend more than 26,000 New Zealand dollars.
Every member of parliament, every candidate
is allowed to spend up to that amount, but you can't legally spend over that.
There isn't.
So I think we've got very tight laws around that.
So, actually, in New Zealand, my story in politics is not very common, actually.
The more common pathway is people coming out of university,
working as staffers in a political party,
or in a government, becoming a member of parliament, then becoming a minister.
Actually, there hasn't been a story of someone like me
coming from outside in the way that I have.
Most countries, I think, have these laws, Chris, even in the US.
But I think political parties find ways to circumvent them.
Yes, I'm just saying to you, in New Zealand,
we have very strict laws as a result.
And you see it in some Scandinavian countries as well,
where that is just not negotiable for us.
-There is just no way for that to happen. -Yeah.
So, you know, there is really tight caps of spending.
It's very, very small amounts of money
relative to people running for office in local government,
even in US cities or towns.
But we like it that way, because it means that then money isn't the reason
for which someone can't run as a parliamentarian.
And if you look at our parliament, what you want to see is diversity.
You want to see people from all walks of life.
As you said, artists, police officers, school teachers,
business people, yeah, we want to see a real diversity.
Yeah.
Who is harder to deal with,
shareholders at your previous role or voters today?
Oh, I think, without a doubt, the public.
I mean, you're dealing with much more substantive issues
about whether they're getting a dividend or return.
The most important thing is actually to improve the lives of New Zealanders.
And so, you know, and they have high expectations of us
to deliver for them and to improve public services,
to grow the economy, to keep them safe, and have good law and order.
So, you know, "I'm here to do the very best I can
with what I know to improve the lot of New Zealand
and my fellow citizens."
And that's what the mission is.
And so, you know, but as a result,
there's huge expectations, and quite rightly so.
Mm-hmm.
I went to the match recently, the India-New Zealand cricket final.
-I know you-- -In Dubai?
-In Dubai. -Oh, my goodness.
I haven't raised at all that 3-0 whitewash test series that we had here.
I actually was down playing a little bit of cricket
at the stadium here in Mumbai just today,
just this morning with Ajaz Patel who took 10 for 119 here.
So, and as a son of Mumbai as well, now in New Zealand.
In the stadium, there were, I don't know, 50,000 people.
49,950 of them were wearing a blue T-shirt.
Yes.
And there were 50 of New Zealanders in the crowd as well.
But that brought me to a very interesting analogy
that I heard somewhere
that the sheep-to-supermodel ratio
is highest in New Zealand.
I have no idea about that.
But what I can tell you is,
yes, look, I mean, we're a country of 5.5 million people.
And we live in an incredible landscape and land called New Zealand.
That's the size of Japan or size of the UK.
-Mm-hmm. -And how lucky are we?
Because with all respect to my Indian friends,
we live in the best country on planet Earth.
And so we're really lucky to live there.
But New Zealanders constantly punch above their weight.
You know, that's the nature of our country.
As if you think about it, because we're at the edge of the world.
I'm sorry, I meant supermodel-to-sheep ratio.
I should have said the other way around.
Either way, I have no idea.
It's not one of the stats that I get to see regularly.
But, you know, really, this is a country that is at the edge of the world,
and, therefore, has a lot to offer the world.
Because it's when you have to be resourceful
that you actually innovate and you end up doing amazing things.
And so, you know, it was Lord Rutherford
that split the atom, you know, out of New Zealand.
It was Kate Sheppard who gave women the vote,
the first country in the world to ever do so by a long time
before others got with the program of equality.
Yeah, think about someone like Sir Peter Beck today,
who's now made New Zealand the fourth biggest launcher of rockets
into space after the US, Russia, and China.
Yeah, very interesting.
Yeah, so when you come from a small country
at the end of the world, that's a huge advantage
because you think different, because you have to think different.
And as a result, that's why you see New Zealanders
whether it's in the field of sports or science or innovation
or entrepreneurship actually doing very well.
As an entrepreneur investor,
if there is one thing that I can do with New Zealand
from the capitalistic lens to benefit, what would it be?
As an Indian entrepreneur.
Well, I think, you know, we were very fortunate.
Actually, just last week we had, actually, 107 organizations
come down from 16 different countries
with six and a half trillion dollars worth of money to invest
to make that exact case to them,
That's why we've just launched actually what we call the Active Investor Visa.
You know, if you want to put $5 million dollars New Zealand
into investment in New Zealand,
there's a pathway to residency in three years.
-And so, it's very-- -Much like the US.
Yes, I mean, they copied us... after us.
But, you know, that sort of proposition doesn't exist
in the UK or Australia now, really.
But that's a great opportunity for us to get that connectivity
because New Zealand, despite our distance and where we are placed in the world,
we are not an inward-looking people,
we're very outward-looking people.
You know, we have one fifth of our country
living overseas on any given day of the week.
So, we are very engaged in the world
despite our remoteness at times or our geography.
And so, you know, that's why, you know, we want to welcome people to New Zealand
so that we can actually get more of those connections together
so that we can actually, you know, deepen the relationships.
Because when you deepen relationships,
you talk about the possibilities of what you and I could do together.
We get to know each other,
then you put some plans together and you get some results.
So, the bigger the relationships, always the bigger the results.
Do you like the US model, Chris?
A lot of, I heard a lot of your interviews over the last many years.
There are many similarities in the direction America is taking
and you seem to be taking.
I even heard that you spoke to Trump recently.
I mean, in terms of him talking about meritocracy,
lesser weightage on DEI,
businessmen in politics, the DOGE getting corporates to come in
and try to fix politics in a very corporate way.
Are there many similarities between what you're doing and what they are?
No, I don't think-- Yeah, we're following our own path.
And, you know, I've been in office now for 16 months,
and we've inherited a really challenging set of economic circumstances.
And we went to our election in New Zealand saying we want to rebuild the economy,
we want to restore law and order,
and we need to deliver better health and education outcomes for New Zealanders.
So yeah, I get some of the parallels people make with any country.
People might make those assumptions, but it's definitely not the case for us.
It's about our own... plotting our own course
and saying what's right for New Zealand.
We want to embrace more science, technology, innovation.
We want world-class infrastructure.
We need to make sure it's an easy place to do business with little bureaucracy.
We need to make sure we attract trade and investment.
Equally, for our most vulnerable citizens,
we want to invest in what we call social investment,
which is actually using our community organizations
and actually get the money out of the bureaucracy,
into those organizations to get better health
and educational outcomes and social outcomes for people.
And obviously, we've got a big commitment to, you know, net zero 2050.
And, in fact, our latest plan,
which we have legislated into our parliament,
it was just, we might even get there six years early.
So we want to be able to deliver on our emissions targets.
We want to be able to make sure we invest
and make powerful interventions in people's lives
so that they get to get a better shot,
and obviously improve the living standards for everybody.
When you said you're into your term halfway almost already,
is it a problem that the term in New Zealand is as short as it is?
Like in India, the incumbent leader
has been in his position for a prolonged duration,
which has given us a certain sense of stability in policy-making,
and even, like, the direction the country will move in.
Is it a problem that the terms are so short?
Yeah, I mean, I, personally, am a supporter
of extending our terms to four years.
I mean, obviously, here in India, you have a five-year term.
The UK has a five-year term.
Australia and New Zealand, I think,
are one of the few countries that have three-year terms.
-Most, on average, have four. -Mm-hmm.
I just think it would give governments more time to deliver,
and then, for voters to ultimately make a decision
as to whether they are or are not delivering.
But having said all of that, that's not a decision that I want to lead.
That should be one that's led by the New Zealand people.
And so, we have a bill going into our parliament now
for extending the term from three to four years.
And it's actually ultimately for the New Zealand people
who will make that decision.
When you spoke to President Trump, can I ask one thing?
I know this is a very tough question to answer,
and maybe I should not ask it,
but one thing you like about him and one thing you do not?
No, I'm not going to go there
because, actually, it's not for me to comment
on my relationships or conversations I have with other world leaders.
I mean, my job and what I enjoy doing
is building a relationship with each of the leaders
that I deal with around the world.
We're all different.
And the US has been a long standing partner of New Zealand
for a very long period of time.
And so, it was very good of President Trump, frankly,
to call me after he'd won the election.
We had a good 20 minute chat.
We talked a lot about, actually, the issues that we see
in the Indo-Pacific region.
And we've subsequently had good interactions
with our foreign ministers and secretary of state
and also our defense ministers as well.
Could you tell maybe... Fair, that question is a complex one
and maybe should not have been asked,
but is there one thing in his form of governance
that you think the world will be served well in adopting?
In President Trump's?
-Look, I'm just really not going to go-- -Do you think DOGE is a good idea?
Well, again, it's about making sure you've got a public...
I'm not going to talk about US domestic politics
because I'm not responsible for the US,
but what I can do is the parallel in New Zealand
is to make sure that my public service is as efficient as it possibly can be,
and that it's focused on delivering for New Zealanders.
And I do think in the public service,
and you think about the bureaucracies that exist
in many countries around the world,
is the question is, how do you make it deliver
so that it's always focused on the people?
And sometimes, that gets lost.
And so, in my case, what I'm doing is making sure
that I want them to think of themselves
as large service organizations that have customers.
If you build customer centricity into any organization,
as you well know from your own background,
we want the public servants of New Zealand to be thinking that they are there
to serve patients who need health care services
and equality access to timely health care.
And we want to make sure that they understand
their job going into work today
is to improve reading and mathematical outcomes for our kids.
We want to make sure that infrastructure is getting built and roads and stuff.
So I've spent a lot of time looking at other small advanced economies.
And if you look at, say, Singapore,
they've got a very effective public service
and I've actually spent a lot of time studying that system.
I think there's a huge opportunity across governments around the world,
but certainly, in my own country, to leverage technology to really make sure
that the public service and the government is delivering for its people.
And I just don't-- I think we've got a lot of opportunity there,
but it's about a customer mentality.
If I were to bring it back to the entrepreneur
turning into the politician...
I don't have intent of being a politician, but for many people like me,
I hope there are more people
going from the corporate world to politics in India,
is there any advice you would like to give?
What skill sets should they focus on acquiring?
Well, I think you have to go being prepared to unlearn things
that may have made you successful in one job, in one career.
Can you elaborate? That's very interesting.
You've got to unlearn some stuff and you've got to learn some new things.
And the way that you run companies
is different from what's involved in politics.
Politics, you're getting into policy areas
that are often very layered, very nuanced, very complex.
And sometimes, you just can't hit those simplistically.
You've got to be able to work your way through it.
Now, your training and your discipline of how to attack a problem
and how to define a problem and put a common sense solution to it,
I think, is something that I've learned from my business life as well,
is that governments don't do problem definition well.
And therefore, the solutions are often roaming around
in search of problems to attach to.
So I think, yeah, from my point of view,
it's being prepared to start from the bottom
and actually say, I'm in a whole new thing.
I have gone from playing cricket to hockey,
and I want to be really great at cricket, which I was,
and now, I'm going to be really great at hockey.
Well, what skills do I have to learn that are different?
And that means mastering the policy areas.
It means understanding how parliament works.
It's being connected to your party,
and most importantly, delivering for people.
And so, you know, that's what I encourage
our new members of parliament to think about as well.
I think it was Malcolm Gladwell who once said that--
-Great podcast, "Revisionist History." -Yeah.
I think he once said that it's probably a good thing
that politicians are not too efficient.
'Cause that way not too much can change at once,
which might be risky for the world.
Yeah, look, I think it comes back to that balance,
which is, if everything's just incrementally
on how we've always done things, it doesn't get you there.
Equally, if it's all chaos and it's everything's turned upside down,
that doesn't get you there.
And what you've really got to do is blend both those things.
Protect and enhance the good stuff, right?
That's actually made a society work really well.
Understand it, understand what's made it successful.
Make sure you enhance, protect it.
But, on the other hand, you've got to challenge and provoke it as well.
Otherwise, you don't get any growth.
You don't position your country,
your organization, your team in the right way.
And so, for me, it is actually getting both those bits right.
Understand how you want to stimulate and provoke challenge,
but equally, protect and enhance the good stuff.
And I think that's a better way to go
because I think the public now, across the world,
and in my experience in New Zealand as well,
is that they want delivery.
They're sick of politicians talking and bumper stickers.
They actually want...
"Help me improve my education. Help me improve my healthcare outcomes.
Help me get rid of the congestion that I'm dealing with.
I'm wasting all this time and traffic.
Build me proper infrastructure, you know.
Grow this economy so I've got more choices and more money in my pocket,
so I can live the life that I want to live."
So that's about delivery. It's about delivery.
Chris, when you were heading Unilever,
if a career politician applied for a job as your deputy, would you hire him?
Well, again, it's not because of what they've done, it's who they are.
So, a lot of politicians really struggle coming out of political life
and doing the switch from hockey to cricket, essentially,
They really don't fit going into the business world
because they're not prepared to do the same things that we just talked about.
They also have to humble themselves and start at the bottom again,
and not just assume that they're gonna be able to
become a director of a company because they've been a politician.
It's not actually about business people going into politics
or politicians coming into the business world.
What you're trying to do,
if you're a community leader leading a nonprofit,
if you are a business leader, as you have been, or I,
or a political leader,
you really want the principles of great organizations
is what you're interested in, and good leadership.
And there are many businesses that are very badly run with very bad CEOs
that actually don't do a very good job at all.
And therefore, just because they're CEOs
doesn't mean they're gonna make great politicians.
It's not about that.
It's about the principles of building great organizations and great leadership.
If I were to generalize what you're saying a little bit,
you're also saying that it's easier for a businessman to transition to politics
than it is for a politician to transition to business,
because the former is more efficient that can be translated into politics?
No, I disagree. I'm saying, in both cases, it's the same.
That, actually, it's not relevant what your job title was
as a business person or a politician,
it's about the individual as to whether they can make that transition themselves.
And really, that transition is about the principles of leadership
and the principles of creating and building great organizations.
And as I said, it's not just about
"Anyone going from the business world is gonna make the political world better."
No, it's not the case. And likewise, going the other way.
It's purely about finding the right people,
that actually... right individuals that have the emotional intelligence,
the skills, the experience to actually do the jobs properly
is what's really... that's much more important.
I can tell you a number of really successful nonprofit organizations
that have outstanding leadership
and are really well-organized, very efficient,
awesomely performing organizations.
I could point to some countries that are run like that.
I could point to some businesses that are run like that and nonprofits.
And so, what's common is the individuals and the leadership principles
and the principles of building good organizations,
not whether they've been from one of those fields
of business or politics.
For someone like me, Chris,
who has no access to world leaders, like you might,
can you teach me what is wrong with the world today?
Why are so many different people fighting with each other at the same time?
Look, I think we're in a really different phase.
I mean, yeah, we've had a world order, essentially,
for the last 70 years, post World War II,
that kind of has kind of worked.
And actually, most countries have done exceptionally well off the back of that.
But you are seeing a shift from rules to power, as I said at Raisina,
you're seeing a shift from economics to security.
-Do you think the world order is changing? -Yeah, I do.
And I think also you're seeing a shift from sort of efficiency to resilience.
And so--
Can you explain that, efficiency to resilience?
Well, essentially, the old model was that we would go find
the lowest-cost products and services in a global supply chain
and actually make sure that we could source them
from anywhere in the world, globally.
COVID disrupted a lot of those supply chains.
And so, as a result, we ended up determining
we need to build more resilience
into each of our individual countries as well.
Economics, you want prosperity,
but to have prosperity, you got to have security.
So, are you saying the commoditized business of creating a product,
which is very close to the commodity,
the cheapest version of it will no longer work,
-but the value add is-- -I'm just saying there's a shift.
You know, there's a shift from...
That was the dominant efficiency model that we were working towards.
And now, what we've realized in a post-COVID world,
where often supply chains got disrupted or conflict disrupts a supply chain,
actually having a degree of resilience is kind of important.
So, you know, that's the macro trends that are happening.
But also you're dealing with an age of disruption
where, you know, populations across the world are angry,
they're frustrated, they feel dislocated, disrupted
for a number of different reasons.
And as a result, they want their politicians to deliver
and improve their daily lives.
Europe is arming itself again, rearming itself.
I read in philosophy or in history that if there are warriors, there will be war.
So where do you think the world is heading towards
if more people are starting to arm themselves all over again?
Will there be more war in the next ten years?
No, not necessarily.
But if you think about, you know, peace through strength,
and you think about this idea of...
Yeah, you just look at the war in Ukraine, right?
That was an illegal immoral war launched by Russia on Ukraine.
And the implication of that is that many people in Western Europe,
actually through high energy prices,
created huge amounts of economic pain and suffering.
Go look at what's happened with grain prices throughout Africa,
even rice prices throughout Southeast Asia.
Huge impact because of that one conflict as a result.
You know, that's why I keep saying,
we don't want to have instability or insecurity in a region.
You've got to make sure that you've got security
and economic prosperity. The two go hand in hand.
If you were to take pen to paper, have a whiteboard,
and redraw government in the manner that will work for tomorrow,
what would you change?
Socialism, capitalism, left, right?
I don't think those labels are really very helpful, to be honest.
-I think what's much-- -How would you label your board?
Well, what I'd label is, it's about being customer-centric.
It's really actually about delivering for your people and their needs.
And when I talk to New Zealanders at the moment,
what's the number one need that they have?
It's about dealing with the cost of living,
improving their economic well-being.
It's restoring law and order, so they feel safe in their homes,
their businesses, their communities.
It's certainly about delivering better health and education outcomes
so their kids have a great future.
So if you stay focused on those things and those goals,
then you drive government to deliver those goals.
And you can talk about left, right, and labels as much as you like,
but actually, the public want you to deliver for them
and actually help them have a better life.
And they get more choices, more freedom
as to what they get to do with their time and their money.
Yeah. I think we have time for one last question.
Chris, what would you say, what would you do
if there was absolutely no fear?
No fear of judgment, repercussions, nothing.
One thing that you feel, but are not able to say.
There's so many things I feel, but I'm not able to say.
Well, I don't feel particularly constrained
because I know what are my mission and purposes at a personal level,
and I know what we're trying to achieve at a country level.
And so, for us, it's like, right, let's go.
There will be trade-offs, I'm sure, along the way,
which, inevitably, you have to make,
but because you're trying to make sure you deliver
for the most amount of people in the fastest possible way.
I think you got to do the very best you can
with what you know at the time and move forward.
I don't even mean about New Zealand or governance or politics.
I just mean about the world in general.
What is one thing that should be said, but is not being said?
Well, I mean, I actually think the world's going to be in good hands.
I think the next generation coming in behind me
is actually, I look at my kids, you know, and I look at their friends.
I think they're incredibly altruistic.
They don't come with the baggage and prejudice
of historical prejudice that often you see
being observed in the generation even ahead of me.
Is altruism real or is it more motivational?
They want to collaborate.
They want to work together in different ways.
There's less prejudice sitting there on historical issues.
And I think that's a good thing.
So, I actually feel very optimistic about the future in the world.
I think, I feel really optimistic about it,
you know, that actually the next generation coming behind us
will lead just as well as,
be the right people for the right time.
I think your generation is definitely optimistic, as is mine,
but if you go to a generation of people born beyond '95 or 2000,
they don't have the same optimism we did or we have today.
Why do you think that is?
Well, my request to them would be get involved.
You know, get involved, you know, change the system.
If you don't like the system, get into the system
and actually make it a better system.
You know, that's what I've tried to do.
You can sit on the sidelines and you can comment,
get behind your keyboard and sound off,
but otherwise, get into the arena and actually give it a go
and actually shape it and make it change,
and be the change that, you know, famously said, you know, you want to see.
So, you know, that's important.
And I just would encourage young people, if you're frustrated with the system,
you know, don't disengage from the system, go towards it
and walk into that system and try and change it.
Thank you, Chris. This was lovely.
-Thank you for taking the time. -It was lovely to be with you.
-I hope you had a good time. -I loved it.
And can I just say to you, come and visit us in New Zealand.
We'd love to host you down there, I'd love to show you around a bit
and show you some things down there, but I think you'd really enjoy it.
-I would love to. -Yeah, I hope to see you there.
-100%. -It's been great.
-Thank you so much. -Appreciate it. Take care.
Thank you, everybody. Have a good rest of the afternoon.
